skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guide for designing, implementing, and evaluating Model Context Protocol servers for external APIs using Python or TypeScript.
skill.summary: Build production-minded MCP servers with protocol guidance, implementation patterns, and an evaluation harness.
skill.author: ArtemisAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity patterns, but most reviewed matches are documentation snippets, Markdown syntax, or Apache license text. The confirmed risk is the bundled evaluation harness, which can launch user-specified commands, connect to user-specified URLs, and pass credentials supplied on the command line. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Evaluation Harness Executes User-Specified Commands
medium_findings.description: The stdio transport accepts a command, arguments, and environment values, then creates StdioServerParameters from those inputs. This is expected for MCP evaluation, but it can run arbitrary local programs if used with untrusted input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct code evidence shows command and args are accepted from CLI options and passed to the MCP stdio client. The behavior is legitimate for an evaluation harness, so the concern is operational misuse rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP Connections Can Carry User-Supplied Headers
medium_findings.description: The SSE and HTTP transports accept arbitrary URLs and headers. This enables testing remote MCP servers, but users can send bearer tokens or custom secrets to endpoints they specify.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code directly parses remote URLs and headers and passes them to SSE or streamable HTTP clients. The endpoint is user controlled, which lowers maliciousness confidence but preserves credential-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Examples Encourage Supplying Secrets on Command Lines
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows API keys and GitHub tokens passed through shell export statements and command-line environment arguments. This is common setup guidance, but secrets in shell history or process listings can be exposed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly includes credential examples, including an API key and a GitHub token placeholder. They are placeholders, not live secrets, so the finding is about unsafe handling patterns.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Reviewed high-severity weak-crypto matches occur in Apache license prose, Markdown text, headings, or ordinary MCP documentation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic algorithms being implemented or invoked by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed these matches are natural-language content or Markdown examples, not cryptographic code. No executable crypto usage was found in the reviewed scripts.

low_findings.title: Most External Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many command-execution matches are fenced examples, XML snippets, or checklist text in reference documentation. They describe how to build or evaluate MCP servers and are not executed by the skill automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visibly documentation blocks, not runtime code paths. The real executable command path is captured separately as a medium finding.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Apache license text, MCP documentation, SDK READMEs, and example API endpoints. No evidence found that the skill silently contacts these URLs without user action.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URL locations are explicit references or examples. They do not show background network calls initiated by the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Process Launch
dangerous_patterns.description: The harness maps CLI command, args, and environment values into the MCP stdio client. Users should only run trusted server commands in a controlled workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The process launch path is explicit and central to stdio MCP testing. It is not hidden, but it is a meaningful execution surface.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Remote Endpoint and Headers
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote transports accept a URL and arbitrary headers for SSE or HTTP connections. This can expose supplied tokens if users connect to untrusted endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly forwards user-provided connection settings to network clients. The risk depends on user configuration, not covert behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams need MCP servers that expose external services safely and clearly. This skill guides design, implementation, and evaluation for Python and TypeScript MCP servers.
actual_capabilities: Explains agent-centered MCP tool design and workflow planning.
Provides Python FastMCP implementation patterns with Pydantic validation.
Provides Node and TypeScript implementation patterns with Zod validation.
Includes guidance for tool annotations, pagination, response formats, and error handling.
Includes an evaluation workflow and Python harness for testing MCP server quality.
Points users to current MCP protocol and SDK documentation for deeper reference.
limitations: Does not generate a complete MCP server without service-specific API details.
Requires users to review external documentation and credentials for each integration.
The evaluation harness can run local commands and should only be used with trusted servers.
Examples are templates and may need updates for newer SDK versions.

use_cases.title: Create a Service Integration
use_cases.description: Plan and build an MCP server that exposes useful workflows from a third-party API.

use_cases.title: Standardize Internal MCP Patterns
use_cases.description: Use the guides to align naming, validation, pagination, and error handling across multiple servers.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Tool Use Quality
use_cases.description: Create evaluation questions and run the included harness to measure whether agents can complete realistic tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic MCP Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for one external service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan an MCP server for a simple API. Identify the best read-only tools, input schemas, and response formats.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Python MCP Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Implement a FastMCP server from an API plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a Python FastMCP server plan for this API. Include Pydantic models, tool annotations, error handling, and pagination.

prompt_templates.title: Build a TypeScript MCP Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Implement a TypeScript server with strict schemas.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a TypeScript MCP server. Include Zod schemas, project structure, tool registration, build commands, and response formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate and Improve an MCP Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create ten evaluation questions for my MCP server, then review the expected answers and identify design improvements.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a project management API.
output_examples.output: A prioritized tool plan with search, list, detail, and workflow actions, plus validation and pagination guidance.

output_examples.input: Review my Python MCP server design.
output_examples.output: A quality checklist covering tool names, annotations, Pydantic models, error handling, response limits, and reusable helpers.

output_examples.input: Create evaluations for a GitHub MCP server.
output_examples.output: Ten read-only evaluation questions with stable answers and notes on how to verify each answer.

best_practices: Design tools around complete user workflows instead of wrapping every API endpoint.
Validate every input with Pydantic or Zod and return concise, structured responses.
Run evaluations with trusted credentials and isolate any server command under test.
anti_patterns: Do not expose broad destructive tools without clear annotations and approval expectations.
Do not return large unfiltered API payloads when pagination or summaries would work.
Do not pass real secrets to untrusted remote MCP endpoints or unknown local commands.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design, implement, and evaluate MCP servers that connect AI agents to external APIs.

faq.question: Does it support Python and TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Python FastMCP guidance and Node or TypeScript MCP SDK guidance.

faq.question: Does it include runnable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Python evaluation harness for testing MCP servers with local or remote transports.

faq.question: Is the evaluation harness safe to run?
faq.answer: Use it only with trusted commands, trusted servers, and scoped credentials because it can launch processes and send headers.

faq.question: Can it design write-capable tools?
faq.answer: Yes, but it emphasizes accurate annotations, clear side-effect descriptions, and safer read-only evaluations.

faq.question: Does it replace official MCP documentation?
faq.answer: No. It references official protocol and SDK documentation and organizes practical implementation steps.
